sentences.text: Everyone shakes when they hold it.
You can't help it.
I shake anyway.
OK, Can you read it?
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You need it?
OK, he wants you to ask questions, I think.
Should I look on her?
She's a--
My name is Esther Kaufman, and I live in Vancouver.
And Kaufman is K-A-U-F-M-A-N.
Esther, E-S-T-H-E-R.
Esther Zuckierman, Z-U-C-K-I-E-R-M-A-N.
No.
October the 11th, 1920.
I would-- I-- 75.
Poland.
And the city is Cracow.
C-K-- no, C-R-A-K-O-W.
My father's name was Josef.
Yes.
J-O-S-E-F, Josef.
He was a businessman.
Yes, he had a wholesale in, in Cracow, textile wholesale.
Lola, L-O-L-A.
Rakowska.
R-A-K-O-W-S-K-A Rakowska, I think so.
She was working with my father in the business.
Very successful, a wholesale of textile.
Yes.
Both.
We're going to the synagogue.
My father was too busy go in the weekdays, so he was going the weekends.
Like Saturday, he was in the synagogue and every holidays.
And my mother was with him.
My mother really was more religious than my father.
But he love his wife, so he did whatever she wanted.
Yes, I have one left, Norman, who lives in Calgary.
And he-- and I lost three-- two, two brothers I lost during the war.
One name was Israel.
And the other one was Moishe.
Yes.
I-- the public school.
And after, I went to business school.
But it was interrupted by the Germans who were-- who came in.
Completed?
The business school wasn't.
It was about two, three years I was going.
No, no, no, not a religious school.
Yes.
And they all disappear.
There's nobody left from them.
No, during-- before the war, no I didn't.
I tell you, in Poland, you-- we were so protected that I couldn't have aspiration because-- I had aspiration to God that my mother and father would be healthy and everything would be-- goes smoothly.
My two brothers got married and had grandchildren.
We were a very close family.
But the aspiration were not there because-- I don't know why.
I can see my grandchildren growing up now.
And it is completely different.
Who was living?
We were--
After?
I was living with my parents, sure.
No, my brothers both were married.
They were separate apartments in different districts.
Yes.
Cracow was in the walking distance, yeah.
Yes, we were very knit-- close family.
Yes, I-- my-- can I?
My, my father had a brother who lived in Cracow.
And I was seeing him quite often.
Oh, That's a good question.
I really don't remember, sorry.
My grandparents lived in Vancouver.
Vancouver, Canada.
And they're buried in the Schara Tzedeck Cemetery.
No, but my mother's whole family lived in Canada, in Vancouver.
She was the only one.
But she married, and she was very successful.
So-- and here, business was very bad.
As a matter of fact, she was sending money and whatever she had-- she could help it-- to the parents here, to Vancouver.
Oh, it must have been the '20-- not in-- maybe before, '14, maybe, 1914, 1915.
No, my mother promised me when I would be certain age we could go.
But never, never came that age.
Yes, I can.
Get up in the morning.
I-- when I was going to public school, I was rush, rush, rush to the school.
And after that was in the afternoon.
So we were coming home for lunch because it wasn't too far to go, and the-- when I was taking the business school.
So it was the afternoon, in the evening.
So and after that, we are going home.
We were close.
We didn't have the freedom what the kids have now.
My coming was-- my mother and father expected me to be home.
Did you have any hobbies or interests?
Not really.
I was very guarded, spoiled.
Just going out with the kids, and sometimes going places where my parents didn't know where I went.
I hope they aren't in another life to see the tape.
Just walking with boys, but under the bridge where nobody can see it.
Yes.
Yeah, we all were, you know, a group of kids going together.
But the parents didn't-- they, they shouldn't have done.
Quiet.
When I had to answer, I had to answer-- respect for the mother and father, and [SIGHS] lots of friends.
That's all.
My mother and father.
They would both know my-- if there was a problem, then they knew about.
Yes.
It was quite religious because we had the Jewish maid, who was cooking that while mother was in the business from 8 o'clock in the morning until late, and eating late, late dinner.
And Shabbos was-- Friday night dinner was, you know, lighting the candles, and-- a typically religious home.
Fish, soup, meat, and vegetables, and that's it, whatever-- pastry.
I didn't, no.
I [SIGHS] didn't feel like to go.
You know, when you grow up, you have something else in mind than to go the synagogue.
I was going with my friends.
We were going out.
But there was a respect for my mother and father.
And when they were coming home, I tried to be home, so-- to give them the respect what they were entitled.
Yes, very much so.
My-- yes, this-- yeah, the oldest brother had one little girl, five years old.
And the non-Jewish people were hiding her during the war.
And the, the youngest had a baby born in the ghetto.
And the German woman killed it, [SOBBING] came with a gun and killed it.
Moishe.
Oh, she is alive.
She lives in Lugano, Switzerland.
And I visit her-- visit her quite often.
Every Sunday, I am phoning her, best sweet girl.
Anne, in Polish was Hanna.
Is gone.
He went with my-- from the ghetto, from-- with my mother and father.
Israel.
He has a son, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shalom.
The rabbi didn't want to come to the birth the other way, if they didn't call him Shalom, 'peace.'
No really.
No, because we are a big family.
And in Poland, we lived-- we had apartment with three bedrooms.
And that was sufficient for my-- for the family.
But holidays, for dinner, they were invited-- we had together.
Very successful.
Very, very wealthy.
I must have, yes.
I was going with my mother because the only time what she could go was really holiday times.
And we were going places where she could observe her holiday.
So she was always taking me with her.
I was bored because I was the only one daughter.
So they always protected me.
In-- like, in a place where she could really have baths and-- like, you know, you go here, and those places to-- like a spa.
In Poland.
No, no, my mother wouldn't let me.
I was too protected to go to those-- mmm.
I had to be certain time at home and not later than 9 o'clock, at 9:30 the latest.
And the other way-- and I wasn't punished because I wasn't the baby anymore.
But I-- the respect was there.
My mother was coming to the house-- if I was sitting, I got up.
My father, the same thing because the respect was really for the mother and father, for the parents.
Yes.
I-- if he did, I really don't know.
Because he was traveling for his business.
Like, he was going to different cities, buying the merchandise, or doing merchandise.
Maybe he did.
But we were never discussing that.
No.
Not really.
We were going, but not too often.
There was one or two movies around, close, Because too far, I was so overprotected, I couldn't go.
And, yeah, I was going to movies Sundays.
Friends, yeah.
We knew war is coming.
But we didn't expect from the German that.
Because I knew there is lots of anti-Semitism in Poland.
But I didn't know that that would be the good part, and the German after, wish they could-- I remember like now.
When they came in, I was standing before the door.
And we lived in an apartment with, like, apartment doors.
And we were greeting them.
We were so happy that they came, the Germans.
Because it was a big anti-Semitism in Poland.
Some-- like my father, who was a businessman and traveled a lot, so for him, it was a relief.
Maybe now it would be better.
He was still scared.
I knew that only from discussion because of myself, I didn't feel nothing.
I didn't have too much contact with non-Jewish people, and so I didn't know too much.
Yes, we knew.
We knew.
Were discussing that, but [SIGHS] you couldn't help it.
Like, I always was discussing with my mother, why don't you go where your whole family is?
Your whole family lives in Vancouver.
Why don't you go?
Why did you stay here?
She said they came to this country from-- because from a small city because of poverty.
And I married well.
And business is good.
And my two sons came into the business.
I cannot leave everything and leave.
One day, we'll go just for a visit.
No, no.
They were too much involved in the business.
That was the tragedy because they could have get the papers to come here to Canada with no problems.
She had, I think, four or five brothers, sisters, and mother and father here, everybody in Vancouver.
So there was no problem.
There was no persecution, really.
We didn't hear nothing.
Poland was the first country where the-- stepped in, in right away to work.
Oh yes.
Oh yes.
It was a Wednesday morning.
I remember like now.
It was around 10 o'clock in the morning when they came in, September the 5th, I think.
I don't remember what I ate yesterday, but that I remember.
But we-- they were greeting from us very nice.
But the next day wasn't so nice anymore.
Which one, the next days, after two days?
The day after-- huh?
The day after, they came to my father.
I remember like now.
No, that was Wednesday when they came.
And so Friday morning, knock on the door.
Can I have your keys?
My father said, from what?
You know, from your business.
And they took the keys from our business.
Our tragedy was very big because my father only believed in the merchandise and only in the business.
He was a businessman.
So home, we had nothing. you know.
We had no money, no nothing.
Everything we had was in the business.
So then they took the keys-- all the businesses.
They took the key, and they left.
And that was the end of the business-- for beginning.
About a week later, they came back and put the-- they didn't know how to open-- was something-- they needed my father.
My father was always scared to go because it was that tragedy already.
So I went.
And they opened the business and came-- and they put the commissar.
Commissar was somebody who took care of-- and all the money, they were collecting.
And I was working with her, with the woman.
She was a German woman.
And she took over the business.
Before us, when I was-- she let me be there.
That was a good thing because I could-- like, one day, I brought a painter.
I said, the business is stealing everything.
Whoever can, steal at night.
So we brought-- I brought a painter, who put merchandise in the ceiling, painted over, and made the hole and hide something.
So we had for after the war when we took it out.
Merchandise, like clothes for-- suits for mens, shirt for men.
We had only good things, really, velvet, you know, better merchandise.
And my father never went back to the business-- not my mother.
I was the only one going there.
And that was the end of it, the end of the business.
After they came to the house and-- for silver.
If we had fur coats, if we had better clothes, they took everything away.
About a week or two later.
It was very rapidly going-- everything.
[LAUGHS] If you didn't give-- so you know that there's no tomorrow.
No, no.
Maybe they had warning, but you, you couldn't imagine that something like it would happen, for no reason that somebody would come to you.
My father was in the business 30 years, or whatever-- that that would be taken away in the second without any warnings, nothing.
So it's-- nobody-- nobody expected that.
After that-- just a moment.
I have to think.
After that was-- I was going for a while to the business and coming home.
We were still living, I don't know, a month or two in Cracow.
And next to Cracow in the mountains, they open a camp, Plaszow.
No, it was a ghetto.
I'm sorry, it was ghetto before.
But that was not far from Plaszow.
And we moved to the-- we moved-- we had to move to ghetto with another one-- two-bedroom apartment with another two families, one on the top of the other.
And we made the best out of it.
And there I got the job on the airport.
So that wasn't too bad because I was never hungry there.
Planes were coming and leaving.
And I had to clean the office.
And that German was very nice to me.
He was giving me to eat, you know, giving me a piece of bread, or whatever.
So that was good then.
And at night, the trucks were taking us back to the ghetto.
But after a while, they liquidated the ghetto.
The came and took us.
No, no, no.
No, no, just went.
Maybe-- I'm sorry to interrupt you.
Maybe we had a little package in the end, every one of us.
But I really don't remember how big the package was or what was in it.
Both from there, we went to the ghetto.
In the ghetto, there was not so much room.
It was small apartment and a few families.
And we lived there for awhile.
If I have to be honest with you, I don't remember the name.
I just remember one girl, who I am very good friends with her still.
She lives in New York.
And her name is Paula Fishman after she married.
We are very close because we spend half the time-- she give me as money for-- helped me to get the job at the airport.
And we were both going to the airport and always, if I could bring her a piece of bread or something, I was bringing to the ghetto.
Oh, sure, sure.
The two, three brother-- two-- three brothers, yeah, and me.
Yes, sure.
One wasn't married.
The youngest wasn't married.
He is younger than I am.
You know, the truth, I don't remember.
I really don't remember how we got it.
But it was tough, you know.
If we went out to, to-- I don't know.
I really don't remember how was it.
No, nobody was working in the ghetto, nobody.
19-- September-- 1940, beginning of 1940.
No, Cracow was liquidated together.
We all went together.
To leave-- to leave--
I don't know if we were the first one or the last ones, but [SIGHS] we-- I was surrounded only by Jews.
So I don't know if they were there-- some before, some came after.
But Cracow was liquidated from the Jews.
Thank you.
